description: LLM-powered semantic field analysis testing Daniel's 22-letter framework against Tanach word usage

holistic_analysis.unifying_concept: deictic and relational expressions, heavily weighted toward divine-human interaction
holistic_analysis.function_word_role: primarily express direct object (את), relative connection (אשר), motion/direction toward divine (אל), and basic human unit (איש)
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Aleph dominate in marking direct objects, relative clauses, and directional motion, forming a relational scaffold for biblical narrative. Content words cluster around divine nomenclature (אלהים, אדני), personal identity (אני, אתה), and unity (אחד), reflecting a theological focus on God, self, and covenantal bonds. This letter's space reveals a foundational layer for expressing monotheistic address, human agency, and interpersonal/divine relations in Hebrew scripture.

holistic_analysis.unifying_concept: spatial, relational, and locative associations (in, at, with, house, son)
content_word_themes: geographical and directional places (land, Jerusalem, wilderness, way)
holistic_analysis.function_word_role: primarily prepositions and adverbs of location (ב + noun = 'in/at house/land/day'), possession ('in hand of'), and time ('in morning/day'), with pronominal suffixes for person reference
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Bet overwhelmingly evoke spatial embedding ('in the house/land/day'), familial ties ('son/daughter'), and possessive relations ('in his hand'), reflecting the preposition ב's core role in Hebrew syntax. Content words cluster around domestic, geographic, and biblical motifs, unifying the space around 'location within' structures or groups. This reveals Bet as a portal to relational containment in Hebrew semantics.

content_word_themes: borders and terrain (גבול, גבעה, גבעת, גיא)
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Gimel overwhelmingly evoke themes of scale, power, and collective entities, from 'great' (גדול) and 'mighty' (גבור) to nations (גוים) and borders (גבול). This lexical space clusters around biblically resonant notions of grandeur and territorial expanse, with many terms doubling as proper names like Gilead (גלעד). Absent function words, the set is purely content-driven, highlighting Gimel's affinity for substantive, high-impact concepts.

holistic_analysis.unifying_concept: relational dynamics and pathways (speech connecting people, paths of movement, blood ties, judgment processes)
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Dalet heavily cluster around speech (דבר forms dominate frequency), pathways (דרך variants), and relational concepts like blood/kinship (דם, דוד) and likeness/appearance (דמות implied). This suggests a semantic space emphasizing connection, movement, and judgment in human interactions. Absent function words highlight a content-rich domain focused on narrative and relational content.

holistic_analysis.function_word_role: primarily definite article 'הַ-' marking specificity, with pronouns הוא/היא/הם/היו expressing identity, existence, and third-person reference
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words beginning with Hei heavily feature the definite article 'הַ-', creating a semantic space dominated by definite, contextually anchored nouns and deictic pronouns that specify 'the/this/that' in reference to people, places, God, and time. Content words cluster around biblical-theological motifs like land (הארץ), people (העם), God (האלהים), and priesthood (הכהן), reflecting a narrative focus on covenant, location, and divine-human relations. Function words reinforce this by providing grammatical definiteness and existential pronouns, distinguishing them as structural anchors rather than thematic carriers.

content_word_themes: Verbal actions (speaking, coming, going, seeing, making)
Spatial and relational positions (upon, to, until)
holistic_analysis.function_word_role: Primarily conjunctions linking clauses (ו + verb/noun) and direct object markers, enabling continuous storytelling
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words beginning with Vav overwhelmingly form narrative chains in Biblical Hebrew, with ו as a prefix driving sequential actions, speeches, and events. Content words cluster around dynamic verbs of motion, perception, and creation, reflecting epic storytelling patterns. Function words reinforce cohesion, contrasting with content words by providing grammatical glue rather than lexical meaning.

Words starting with Zayin heavily feature demonstratives like זה and זאת, anchoring reference and distinction in discourse, alongside themes of male identity (זכר), ritual sacrifice (זבח), and separation (זרע as seed/progeny, זר as stranger). Gold (זהב) stands out as a symbol of value, while recurring names and remembrance terms suggest historical and patriarchal emphases. This creates a word-space of pointing outward, distinguishing self from other, and preserving male lineage through ritual and memory.

content_word_themes: military conflict and ruin (חרב, חיל, חמס, חמת, חרון)
life and living (חי, חיים, חיי, חיה)
sin and moral failing (חטאת, חטא, חטאתי)
strength and favor (חזק, חזקיהו, חסד, חן, חסדו, חסדך)
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Chet reveal a rich semantic space dominated by themes of life (חי, חיים), war/destruction (חרב, חיל), and moral/ethical concepts like sin (חטא) and grace (חסד), alongside wisdom (חכמה) and strength (חזק). Numerical terms like חמש ('five') add a quantitative dimension but do not unify the set. This distribution suggests Chet-initial vocabulary clusters around human existential, martial, and spiritual experiences without a single overriding concept.

Words starting with Tet heavily cluster around concepts of moral goodness (טוב) and ritual purity/impurity (טהור/טמא, טהרה), reflecting biblical emphases on ethical and cultic states. Sensory and natural themes like taste (טעם), dew (טל), and predation (טרף) add layers of physical and primal experiences. This reveals a word-space blending abstract moral judgments with tangible purity rituals and nature, with no function words to dilute the content focus.

word_details.english: he will be put to death

content_word_themes: Judah and Jerusalem as central places
Verbal actions (know, give, go out, sit, come)
holistic_analysis.function_word_role: יהוה acts as the primary divine name (proper noun, not typical particle); ישראל denotes the nation/people, serving as a core identifier rather than a grammatical function word
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Yud overwhelmingly cluster around core Biblical entities: God (יהוה), Israel (ישראל), Judah, Jerusalem, and key figures like Jacob and Joshua, reflecting Tanakh's narrative focus. Temporal terms like יום and ימים provide chronological structure, while verbs emphasize divine/human actions and knowledge. This reveals Yud's word-space as a nexus of sacred history, identity, and prophecy in Hebrew scripture.

holistic_analysis.function_word_role: express causality (כי), totality (כל), and comparative relations
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Kaf heavily feature function words denoting cause, totality, and manner, which dominate frequency and frame relational semantics. Content words scatter across concrete nouns like money, vessels, body parts, and honor-related terms, with minor biblical geography. This reveals a word-space anchored in connective logic rather than a tight thematic cluster.

content_word_themes: heart and inner self (לב, לבו, לבי)
familial and social groups (לבני, לבן, למשפחתם)
holistic_analysis.function_word_role: primarily express negation (לא), possession/dative (לו, לי, לך, להם, לכם, לנו), spatial preposition (לפני, לפניו), purpose (למען), and conditionality (לבלתי, לכן)
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words beginning with Lamed overwhelmingly cluster around prepositional functions linking entities, actions, and divine references, forming a relational web of possession, direction, and purpose in biblical Hebrew. Content words evoke sustenance, emotion, and social structures, often prefixed to nouns, while function words dominate frequency as indispensable connectors. This reveals Lamed as the nexus of syntactic and semantic linkage in the language's high-frequency lexicon.

holistic_analysis.unifying_concept: origins, separation, and authority in biblical narratives
content_word_themes: biblical geography and exodus (מצרים, מואב, מדבר, ממצרים)
objects and actions (מים, מזבח, מלחמה, מקום, מעשה)
holistic_analysis.function_word_role: primarily prepositions and particles denoting source, separation (מן, מאת, מפני, מעל), possession (ממנו, ממני, ממך), location (ממצרים, משם), quantity (מכל, מבית, מבני), and interrogation (מה, מי)
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words beginning with Mem heavily populate biblical themes of royal authority, exodus from Egypt, and spatial/temporal origins, reflecting narrative motifs of movement and hierarchy. Function words emphasize relational dynamics like 'from/out of' and possession, contrasting with content words' focus on proper names, places, and concrete entities. This reveals Mem's word-space as a nexus for separation, provenance, and leadership in Hebrew scripture.

Words beginning with Nun heavily cluster around נתן ('gave') and its conjugations, indicating a core theme of bestowal and provision, alongside נפש ('soul') forms emphasizing personal vitality and inner life. Prophecy and divine speech emerge via נא, נאם, and נביא, while concepts of rest (נח), inheritance (נחלה), and descent (נפל) suggest patterns of settling, falling, or positional change. This reveals a semantic space rich in transfer, embodiment, and authoritative expression.

Words starting with Samekh reveal a diverse semantic space dominated by concrete nouns tied to biblical contexts, including spatial terms like סביב (around) and סוף (end), everyday objects such as ספר (book) and סלה (basket), and animals like סוס (horse). Proper names (e.g., סיני, סדם, סיסרא) heavily influence the set, reflecting Torah frequency patterns without strong clustering. Absent function words highlight a content-heavy profile centered on narrative and material elements.

holistic_analysis.unifying_concept: relational dynamics (with people, God, place, time)
holistic_analysis.function_word_role: spatial/temporal relations: על (on, upon, about) for position/relation; עד (until, to) for limits/endpoints
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Ayin-initial words cluster around human relations—especially servitude (עבד forms), communal ties (עם variants), and divine interactions—infused with quantitative (עשר) and temporal (עוד, עת) markers. Function words like על and עד frame these as locative/temporal scaffolds, while content words evoke action, place, and hierarchy. This reveals a relational 'word-space' emphasizing connection, obligation, and progression in biblical Hebrew.

content_word_themes: physical actions of opening and bursting
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Pe (פ) reveal a diverse semantic space heavily weighted toward human anatomy (faces, mouths), biblical antagonists like Pharaoh and Philistines, and dynamic actions such as opening or breaking forth. No single unifying concept binds them, but clusters evoke narrative elements from Torah stories involving Egypt, enemies, and divine encounters. The absence of function words underscores a focus on concrete nouns and verbs driving historical and expressive content.

holistic_analysis.unifying_concept: Biblical motifs of divine authority, justice, and conflict
content_word_themes: Righteous figures and justice (צדיק, צדק, צדקה)
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Tsadi heavily populate biblical domains of warfare (צבאות, צבא), moral righteousness (צדיק, צדק), and divine commands (צוה, צו), reflecting Tanakh's core narratives. Clusters around ציון and צור evoke sacred geography and strength, while distress terms like צר add tension to themes of trial and redemption. Absent function words highlight a content-rich lexical space tied to theological and historical concepts.

holistic_analysis.unifying_concept: sacred ritual, divine communication, and spatial/temporal boundaries
content_word_themes: temple holiness and offerings (קדש, קדוש, קרבן)
borders and harvest ends (קצה, קץ, קציר)
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words starting with Qof heavily cluster around Temple worship, with sanctity (קדש forms) and sacrificial offerings (קרבן) dominating alongside divine calls (קרא, קול). Spatial and temporal edges (קצה, קץ) blend with actions of rising (קום) and approaching (קרבו), suggesting a conceptual space of sacred convergence and ritual boundaries. This reflects Qof's phonetic gravity in evoking holiness, proclamation, and liminal transitions in biblical Hebrew.

Words beginning with Resh (ר) reveal a diverse semantic space dominated by concepts of leadership (head), perception (seeing), moral duality (evil vs. companionship), numerical abundance, and ethereal movement (wind/spirit). No single unifying concept binds them, but clusters around human experience—authority, sight, morality, quantity, and motion—highlighting Resh's role in denoting relational and perceptual dynamics in biblical Hebrew. Proper names and body parts further ground the set in narrative and physicality.

Words beginning with Shin cluster heavily around personal and divine names, numerical quantification, and temporal markers, reflecting core biblical concerns with genealogy, chronology, and counting. Themes of peace, hearing (obedience), and leadership further emphasize relational and authoritative structures in Hebrew scripture. This lexical space reveals Shin as a nexus for identity, order, and covenantal themes.

holistic_analysis.unifying_concept: 2nd person commands and predictions of action
content_word_themes: personal actions (eat, do, go, take, speak)
holistic_analysis.semantic_summary: Words beginning with Tav overwhelmingly cluster around 2nd person singular/plural imperatives and future tense verbs, forming a directive and prophetic 'word-space' of commands and predictions central to biblical narrative. Sensory verbs like see, hear, fear reinforce themes of divine instruction and obedience, while spatial terms (under, within) and moral concepts (perfect, abomination) add layers of relational and ethical positioning. This reveals Tav as a hub for agency, perception, and covenantal imperatives in Hebrew scripture.
